label: 50_turnout_voters_harris_democratic
description: Analysis of voter participation in recent U.S. elections shows significant increases among certain demographics like Asian Americans and Latinos, while non-college white voters also saw notable changes. These trends are crucial for understanding political shifts and campaign strategies.

earliest_articles.title: Why Minorities Will Decide the 2012 U.S. Election
earliest_articles.excerpt: With Republican Mitt Romney now his party’s presumptive presidential nominee, both his campaign and President Obama’s re-election effort are barnstorming the nation for votes. For former Massachusetts Gov. Romney, this means recapturing the enthusiasm of the 2010 midterm GOP rout, especially among white Republican…

earliest_articles.title: On Election Day, A New American Mainstream
earliest_articles.excerpt: Tuesday’s election might someday be seen as a historic turning point in American politics: the last time a major party candidate could hope to win the presidency with a campaign aimed directly at the nation’s “mainstream” white population. If that was Mitt Romney’s strategy, he succeeded wildly, gaining a nearly…

newest_articles.title: 2025 election wrap-up: Insights from Brookings
newest_articles.excerpt: From New York City’s historic mayoral contest to decisive Democratic wins in Virginia and New Jersey, the 2025 off-year elections provide early indicators of voter sentiment and party dynamics as we head toward the 2026 midterms and beyond. In this analysis, Brookings scholars E.J. Dionne Jr., Elaine Kamarck, Darrell…

newest_articles.title: Why Zohran Mamdani’s victory matters: How it happened, what it means
newest_articles.excerpt: “If I can make it there, I’ll make it anywhere.” Frank Sinatra’s buoyant declaration in his classic “New York, New York” may apply to many fields. It has limited application to national politics, particularly to those who rise to become mayor of New York City. Not one person elected to what’s often called the second…

central_articles.title: New voter turnout data from 2022 shows some surprises, including lower turnout for youth, women, and Black Americans in some states
central_articles.excerpt: The 2022 midterm election results made plain that the predicted “red wave”—in which Republicans were expected to win by huge margins in the House of Representatives and take over the Senate—did not occur. In fact, Democrats gained an additional Senate seat, and while Republicans took control of the House, it was by a…

central_articles.title: Turnout in 2020 election spiked among both Democratic and Republican voting groups, new census data shows
central_articles.excerpt: The analysis in this post derives from the Census Bureau’s Voting and Registration supplement, released on April 29. These data are estimates, and may differ from vote counts reported by states. Contents: - Turnout surged among Asian American, Latino or Hispanic, and non-college white voters - Gen Z propelled a surge…
